meta.title: NLIHC Out of Reach — Colorado Housing Wage Benchmarks
meta.report_name: Out of Reach: The High Cost of Housing
meta.methodology: NLIHC's Out of Reach calculates the 'housing wage' — the hourly wage a full-time worker (40 hr/wk × 52 wk/yr = 2,080 hr/yr) needs to afford the HUD Fair Market Rent (FMR) for a 0/1/2/3/4-bedroom unit without spending more than 30% of income on housing. Formula: housing_wage = (FMR × 12) / (0.30 × 2080) = FMR × 0.01923. NLIHC also reports renter median hourly wage, % of renter households unable to afford the FMR, and the work-hours-per-week needed at minimum wage to afford a 2-BR. Published annually at the state, MSA, and county level for all 50 states. For Colorado, NLIHC publishes a state report each June + supplements with the underlying county CSV.
meta.important_disclaimer: Values below are COHO's best-effort directional estimates for the most current report year. For policy briefs, news, or grant applications, ALWAYS pull the latest NLIHC Out of Reach publication directly — values change annually with HUD FMR updates each April. The methodology (2080-hour year, 30% affordability threshold, current FMR) is fixed; only the FMR + state minimum wage inputs change yearly. Colorado minimum wage for 2026 = $14.81/hr (CO Dept of Labor + Employment).

state_summary.narrative: A Colorado renter needs to earn $38.42/hr full-time to afford the statewide 2-BR FMR without rent burden. Median renter wage is $22.50 — leaving a $15.92/hr gap. At Colorado's 2026 minimum wage ($14.81), a worker would need to work 104 hr/wk year-round to afford a 2-BR — i.e., 2.6 full-time jobs.

08013.notes: Boulder MSA — UC research economy elevates median wage but FMR is high.

08029.notes: Western Slope rural — lowest housing wage in the state.

08035.notes: Denver MSA — Highlands Ranch / Castle Rock.

08037.notes: Vail / Edwards / Avon resort — workforce housing crisis. 30%+ gap.

08045.notes: Glenwood Springs / New Castle / Silt. Resort spillover; Silt + New Castle commuter sheds for Glenwood + Aspen workforce.

08059.notes: Denver MSA — Lakewood / Wheat Ridge / Arvada.

08069.notes: Fort Collins / Loveland — research / brewery economy.

08077.notes: Grand Junction / Fruita. Western Slope hub.

08097.notes: Aspen / Snowmass — highest housing wage in the state. Workforce-housing crisis at a level only Manhattan exceeds nationally.

08117.notes: Breckenridge / Frisco / Silverthorne / Dillon resort cluster.

by_county._note: Subset of 26 most-populated CO counties shown above. NLIHC publishes data for all 64 CO counties + the statewide aggregate. For counties not listed here, use the state-summary aggregate of $38.42/hr as a directional floor.

lihtc_dev_implications.what_this_says: The 'housing wage' is the bridge between LIHTC-eligible income (≤60% AMI) and the rents your building can charge. Counties where the housing wage gap is high (Pitkin $42, Telluride $34, Eagle $30, Summit $31) are workforce-housing markets — workers earn well above LIHTC limits but can't afford market rent. LIHTC at 80%/100% AMI tiers becomes essential, and the project's competitive set is workforce / attainable rental, not deep affordable.
lihtc_dev_implications.rural_vs_resort_implication: In rural CO (Delta $5.90 gap, Huerfano $6.10, Mesa $7.50), the housing wage is genuinely affordable to median renters. LIHTC 30%/50%/60% AMI works because real local incomes meet that bar. In resort CO (Pitkin $42 gap, Telluride $34, Summit $31), even 80%/100% AMI tiers cap below the workforce's actual income, so workforce-LIHTC and middle-income tools (CO state credit, Prop 123, fee waivers) are needed to fill the cap-stack gap.
lihtc_dev_implications.cite_in_ic_packet: When building an IC memo for a CO LIHTC deal, the NLIHC Out of Reach housing wage is the single best one-line answer to 'why does this jurisdiction need affordable housing?' Pulls a hard number with a national-coalition source.
